Yes, `unsafe_hash` is in use in several other places to ensure `__hash__` is generated when the dataclass is not frozen. Here the dataclass is not frozen because we have a convenience constructor.

As per the [dataclasses documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html), contrary to the what an ordinary interpretation of the word "unsafe" might be, `unsafe_hash` does *not* mean "define a hash which itself is unsafe," `unsafe_hash` instead just means to define the `__hash__` special method which is not defined automatically when the dataclass is not frozen. Thus, "unsafe" here means "Yes Python, I know what I am doing and all of the dataclass attributes are hashable, please define the __hash__ special m method."

You are using MindsDB's files database here, but you should use a Mongo database (instead of connecting MindsDB's files database to itself).

Ideally, you should have your Mongo database (for example, locally), then upload the data file to your Mongo database. And at this step, you should connect your (local) Mongo database to MindsDB - instead of connecting MindsDB's files database.

So here is the summary of the steps:
1. Create a Mongo database and upload your `Date fruit` data file there.
2. Connect MindsDB to Mongo Compass - you did it correctly.
3. In Mongo Compass, use the insertOne method to connect your Mongo database to MindsDB, like this:
```
db.databases.insertOne({
    name: "mongodb-test", // name of the database
    engine: "mongodb", // database engine to use
    connection_args: {
            "port": 27017, // connection port
            "host": "mongodb://localhost:27017", // connection host
            "database": "db_name"  // connecting database
    }
});
```

To clarify, this is a trade-off between two types of overhead: spurious wakeups from sleep, and additional mutations to the linked list of timers. The old method, which maintained the invariant the `mTimer` is set to the expiry time of the earliest TCP timer requires fewer wakeups from sleep, but requires more linked list mutations to maintain that invariant.

If later on, we decide that we want to minimize the number of times `mTimer` fires for performance reasons, at the expense of mutating the linked list more frequently by setting and cancelling `mTimer`, the best way to do that is to add a single call to `ResetTimer` after processing each input packet. That way, traversing the linked list of endpoints can happen once per input packet, not once each time TCPlp sets a timer (which happens multiple times per input packet). I've added a comment in the code to this effect, in case we revisit this point earlier.

Well, export is more about exporting internal information, for consumption by other tools, such as IDEs. We'd export codegen for that reason only - in normal use codegen is a build byproduct that is not visible to the end user. But since the user may want to manually inspect it, and may want to view it in their IDE, we'd export it. 

Documentation, though, is a build product, like a PEX file or a distribution or a docker image or whatever. It is an actual formal output of the system that you can request. So I don't think `export` is the right place for it, just as we don't use `export` to get compiled code, or any other build product.

The two point approximation worked well when the module was tilted at 20-degrees as in the paper. See figures 7, 8, 9, & 10.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1385621/142518429-3c81d83a-9883-4d32-b013-4d199ba511ee.png)

In my opinion, there's no discontinuity. In the figures above, the view factor smoothly goes from 1 to a value infinitesimally less than one at an infinitesimally small distance up the module.

We did not discover the issue until we added trackers, which rotate from east to west and are horizontal in the afternoon. When the modules are horizontal, the view factor is not linear. It's more like a U shape and symmetrical, _EG_: the left and right sides of the module have the same view factor, but the middle is different, so any attempt to approximate it as linear will fail. That's when we realized our error.

I'm fine with splitting the denominator out. That makes it more readable and easier to explain and understand. Thank you for extending the algorithm with the discretized integral. The two point method is wrong and should not be used.

FYI: an alternate method that I'd like to explore one day is crossed strings, which would yield the view factor from one surface to another without integration.

For example, for the `static_partitioner` we know that all subranges are of the same size, except for one subrange that should not differ by one iteration. We also know that ideally these subranges should be executed by different threads and we also know in what circumstances that ideal execution might not happen so we can detect such situations in the test and still make it pass, and then, perhaps, check that non-ideal execution does not happen too often. Merci pour ces explications 🙂 

Comme vu ensemble, cette PR apporte deux changements sur `cmu_forfait_logement_base` : 
* la mise en cohérence de l'appel aux paramètres : 
  * on appelle les paramètres du RSA maintenant et ceux du RMI dans le passé 
   => ✅ ils étaient mélangés en un fichier avant. Ca ne change rien au calcul de répartir ça dans deux yaml.
* L'introduction d'une date de changement de calcul avec `formula_2009_06_01` : 
  * le 1er juin 2009 qui correspond, en résumé, au moment du remplacement du RMI par le RSA. 
  => 🤔 Mais il s'agit de la date de l'entrée en vigueur du RSA en métropole. La question était donc : 
    *  est-ce que `cmu_forfait_logement_base` est appelée dans des calculs des autres régions de France avant cette PR ? Ajouter la date métropole affecterait alors le calcul des autres régions. 
    * ou était-elle déjà limitée à la métropole ? Introduire la date ne changerait alors pas le calcul.

**Alors, alors ?**

Après vérification, nous avons conclu que : `cmu_forfait_logement_base` représentait déjà le cas métropole avant cette PR. Et que pour éviter ces questionnements dans le futur, on allait le préciser dans le libellé.

**Comment est-ce qu'on a vu ça ?**

On sait qu'on a ces dates de transition : 
* 01/06/2009 : RMI -> RSA en métropole
* 01/01/2011 : RMI -> RSA dans les départements et collectivités d’outre-mer
* 01/01/2012 : RMI -> RSA à Mayotte

Or, le [paramètre du montant socle de RMI/RSA](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/f4949d325b01b9bd49353744bc38d79fc5dfa4d8/openfisca_france/parameters/prestations_sociales/solidarite_insertion/minima_sociaux/rmi/rmi.yaml) qui est remplacé dans cette PR par des paramètres distincts RMI/RSA progressait de façon continue. 
En particulier, ses montants de RSA sont de l'ordre de 559.74 ([décret](https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/loda/id/JORFTEXT000038435904/)) alors qu'il est d'un autre ordre de grandeur à Mayotte sur la même période (279,87 d'après [le décret 2019-401](https://mayotte.deets.gouv.fr/sites/mayotte.deets.gouv.fr/IMG/pdf/jo_pdf_frame.pdf) trouvé via [deets.gouv.fr](https://mayotte.deets.gouv.fr/Revalorisation-du-Revenu-de-solidarite-Active-a-Mayotte)).

Donc avant cette PR, on ne traitait pas du cas de Mayotte. 
Est-ce que l'on traitait des départements et collectivités d’outre-mer ? 
Il semble que ce n'était pas le cas au regard de la valeur 2001, valeur de métropole quand, sur la même période, en outre mer `les bénéficiaires du RMI perçoivent 87 % du montant de l'allocation versée en métropole` d'après [ce rapport 3320](3320) de l'Assemblée.

➡️ Conclusion : Avant cette PR, les valeurs du RMI/RSA socle étaient celles de la métropole. Les formules les appelant étaient donc aussi limitée au périmètre de la métropole (sauf appel explicite à un autre paramètre selon la géographie).

```python
    def batch_standard_scaler(df: pd.DataFrame):
        def column_standard_scaler(s: pd.Series):
            s_mean = stats[f"mean({s.name})"]
            s_std = stats[f"std({s.name})"]
            return (s - s_mean) / s_std

        cols = df.columns.difference(["label"])
        return df.loc[cols].transform(column_standard_scaler)

> Would it be reasonable to expect all parameters to have a default?

IMO, yes. We shouldn't have a dataset that is not loadable with `datasets.load("foo")`. Looking through our old datasets, I think there two different scenarios where this violated:

1. Datasets that cannot be automatically downloaded and need multiple files as input

    https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/44e93beb0833ff51ffe75d994fe660434335b53d/torchvision/datasets/coco.py#L28

2. Datasets with arguments that could very well have a default but don't for an probably unknown reason

    https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/44e93beb0833ff51ffe75d994fe660434335b53d/torchvision/datasets/mnist.py#L293

Since the download is handled differently and we no longer need to pass paths to the dataset, I currently don't see a reason to not assume it. If there ever is a case where we actually have no good default value, we can probably hack around it.

> Also, about the need for accessing meta-data without a config: perhaps we don't have that yet, but I could see a dataset that has different classes (or different "extra" field) depending on a parameter. Are we supporting this use-case?

Currently this is not supported. But I'm not sure what would be a good way to add this. With the design discussed in this thread, `datasets.info()` would give you the `Dataset` object instantiated with default parameters. Thus, we would always get the same meta data back. One way would be to accept `**options` there.

`EnvironmentError` is still there but has been merged into `OSError` since Python 3.3 (released in 2012):

> _Changed in version 3.3:_ [`EnvironmentError`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#EnvironmentError), [`IOError`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#IOError), [`WindowsError`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#WindowsError), [`socket.error`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket.error), [`select.error`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/select.html#select.error) and `mmap.error` have been merged into [`OSError`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#OSError), and the constructor may return a subclass.

https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#OSError

There are currently no plans to deprecate/remove `EnvironmentError`, but it's cleaner to use `OSError` everywhere.

For the purpose of hygiene and testability, I'd recommend moving environment variables into an `Environment` struct, and having one global instance of the environment struct rather than many global `Variable` structs. 

Then, it's optional whether the `Environment` object could be initialized in an `init` function or at the top of `main`. 

The primary benefit IMO is that you can inject the `Environment` object into functions that require one or more env vars; this lets you test control flow that uses environment variables without having to test them in your tests (which can be error-prone, since it makes the test impure).

Haskell has it, but so does Python with `typing.NewType` https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#newtype. 

The concept is "I have some pre-existing type I like like `int`, but I want to create a new distinct type that acts like `int` but which must be explicitly used like `MyIntType`." 

The benefit of referencing `newtype` is that devs who already know that concept now instantly make the connection between their prior knowledge and Pants. Maybe calling it `new type` (with a space) + still explaining it, like this, would help people who still aren't familiar?

> The engine makes heavy use of the concept of _newtypes_ to disambiguate similar-looking types. For example, you might want to use `int` in several distinct domains, like `UserId` vs. `ProjectId`. New types allow you to avoid accidentally using `UserId` when you meant `ProjectId`, because each is now a distinct type.

So, the lowdown on generators in Python...

You're surely familiar with using `yield` to write iterator-type functions, but `yield` is actually far more flexible in Python: generator objects (typically created from functions that have `yield` in them) can additionally be _sent_ values, and also _return_ a value when they are done generating.

Generators in Python can be used to express [coroutines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coroutine); see [PEP342](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0342/) for more details.

Anyway, to answer your questions:
- The return type of a function with `yield` in it [is indeed `Generator[yield_type, send_type, return_type]`](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/kinds_of_types.html#generators).
- The return type that was written there previously _is_ correct, but there are currently incompatibilities that arise when you try to use that `Generator` return type annotation, `mypy`, `typeguard`, and the `@contextlib` decorator — it's not possible at present to write a return type hint there that satisfies both `mypy` and `typeguard`.

We can ignore `key` here since the pivots will already have `key` applied to them in the `pick_pivots_from_sample` function.

This code is just to make it so that we split our data correctly if `ascending=False`. If we are dealing with a numeric dtype, I can pass in a reverse sorted list of pivots to `np.digitize`, and it will work. If we are dealing with an object type, say `str`, passing in a reverse sorted list will break `np.searchsorted`:
```python
In [35]: np.searchsorted(["cat", "at"], "bat")
Out[35]: 2

In [36]: sorted(["bat", "cat", "at"])
Out[36]: ['at', 'bat', 'cat']

In [37]: sorted(["bat", "cat", "at"])[::-1]
Out[37]: ['cat', 'bat', 'at']
```
So in order to handle the case when `ascending=False` on a numeric dtype, I simply reverse the pivots. To handle the `ascending=False` case on an object dtype, I need to get the new partition indices as if `ascending=True`, and then reverse them (hence the `groupby_col = len(pivots) - groupby_col` below).

Agreed that I have through a couple reviews been pushing for more explicitness. Much of this derives from my lack of experience with PEX, making it sometimes challenging for me to figure out what certain CLI flags represent or an abbreviated variable stands for. Another motivation is personal style, that to me it seems more readable. I recognize, however, that with open source, codebase conventions should trump personal preferences.

You both raise solid arguments for `_` over `_unused_but_named`. For how I reason through code, the latter style helps me to infer what the function is doing. However, the former style is completely valid and there are strong arguments for why it is preferred. Especially because PEX uses the convention of `_`, I think you're both right that we should stick to that style, or refactor it as John did with the two uses I flagged.

The one point I will continue to advocate for is long CLI flags. Even if that is not the convention with PEX, I always think the readability of long flags trumps saving a few characters for short flags. When using the command line directly, I use short flags, but when writing it in a file saved to VCS, I advocate for long flags.

The `import graphql` statement at the top of the file ensure that these modules can be found in sys modules (all patched functions are imported in `graphql.__init__.py` either directly or indirectly but this could change in a future release). That's why the current approach works.

The main issue with using `__import__()` is that [graphql.graphql](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core-legacy/blob/v2.0.0/graphql/graphql.py) is a module and [graphq.graphql()](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core-legacy/blob/v2.0.0/graphql/__init__.py#L28) is a function in `graphql.__init__.py`. 

Because of this: 
`__import__("graphql.__init__") == __import__("graphql") == __import__("graphql.graphql")` 

To avoid this issue this issue I use sys.modules to get the reference to the `graphql.graphql` module.

>the TF is an API on the Dataset and is a transformation not consumption/sink, it doesn't have two similar "*_size" parameters as well

>If they have such difference, why jam them together, i.e. would it be cleaner to decouple them into separate APIs?

TensorFlow Datasets has batching and shuffling (and prefetching) as separate operations; they can do this since TensorFlow Datasets are lazy by default. For Ray Datasets to support this combination efficiently with an eager-by-default execution model, these arguments need to be specified together, within the consumption API call. And since they are configuring how the data is going to be iterated over, I think this is reasonable until we move to a lazy-first execution model.

I should also note that this API has led to a lot of user confusion, bugs, and performance issues. E.g. batching before shuffling will batch and then shuffle the order of those batches (but not shuffling the rows within or between batches), which is rarely what the user wants (an Anyscale customer ran into this). If you batch upstream, you therefore have to unbatch, shuffle, and rebatch to get the desired shuffling semantics, which is very inefficient.

Consolidating those arguments to a single "convert the dataset to an iterator" API is more declarative, which allows us to:
- avoid these API call ordering bugs and user confusion
- make the shuffler batch-aware, which allows us to optimize for minimal data copying, better batch size + shuffle buffer size validation at API call time, etc.

>the Torch seems a sampler that may use shuffle underneath (not a good idea).

The sampler is a random access (index-based) sampler, so it's a shuffler that generates random indices and yields items with O(1) access for map-style datasets. The Torch example is a bit different, but it illustrates a common theme: users like to think about the size of the set out of which samples will be drawn in terms of samples, even if batching is taking place.

>The examples seem not quite the same as this API:

The Petastorm API is probably the most direct comparison, in which both the batch size and the shuffle queue capacity is specified in the same API, and the shuffle queue capacity is given in number of samples, not as a batch multiplier.

When a message is dropped (see the dispatcher's drop() method) as a result of ack, nack, or drop by the user, messages get released in two ways:
1) dispatcher.drop() calls activate_ordering_keys(), which releases the next message for each ordering key (if they exist). Since at least one message was dropped for each key, it's safe to release a message for each key.
2) dispatcher.drop() then calls maybe_resume_consumer(), which tells the MessagesOnHold class to release the next messages in the queue (within load parameters). These messages may be ordered or unordered.

In your example, the message with the 11th ordering key would be released in step 2.

@fcollonval Sorry, but I don't follow. I included comments explaining why the branch menu listens for each of the three events.

Listening for a `head` change is not enough because, if I create a new branch from the current branch and do so outside of JupyterLab, the `HEAD` does not change, and, thus, the branch change does not get reflected in the UI. The signal which does get emitted when the branch changes, but the `HEAD` does not change, is the `status` signal.

More of an edge case, but a repository change does not necessarily entail a `HEAD` change either. If I had two clones of the same repository sitting side-by-side, I could switch repositories, but still point to the same hash.

Yes, this does mean that multiple event listeners could be triggered, potentially even all 3; however, per the implementation proposed in this PR, this should not trigger a re-render unless model state changes in the interim. In which case, the perf cost is likely to be negligible, and I think acceptable in order to account for edge cases, particularly when mixing Git management inside and outside of JupyterLab (which is something I do regularly).

IMO, I don't think we should have a separate poll for refreshing a list of branches, as multiple polls at different intervals leads to synchronicity issues. And further, even if we polled `refreshBranch`, I don't think that that method should emit a `headChanged` signal, particularly if the `HEAD` has not changed.

Lastly, this PR intentionally eschews the mixed data flow model, as mentioned in the OP and as was used previously, where child components rely on both unidirectional data flow (via properties) **and** querying the underlying extension model (e.g., using getters). Such a mixed model leads to synchronicity bugs, as encountered during development.

Instead, the implementation proposed in this PR relies exclusively on the model (and not parent components) as the source of truth. Moving toward a poll method would most likely entail reverting back to a mixed data flow (e.g., see the `HistorySideBar` component) where, presuming `refreshBranch` does not emit a `HEAD` change event, we'd have to set a `branches` property on the branch menu component to ensure that the UI does not drift from the underlying Git repository.

Furthermore, again presuming that we'd not move toward relying on polling and emitting `HEAD` change events, the fact that changing a repository currently invokes `refresh` (which invokes `refreshBranch`) is only relevant to the implementation currently proposed by this PR insofar as the model's `_branches` property is updated, which the branch menu subsequently accesses after receiving one (or more) signal(s) that the list of branches has potentially changed.

In short, if potentially redundant signals is an overarching concern, I'd be open to throttling, but, in general, I am not particularly inclined to move toward polling or removing listeners which would potentially leave the UI in a state which deviates from the underlying Git repository.

This PR doesn't actually implement the proposal in issue #850. What we need is that the lowest bin _center_ is at `k_min` (its left edge would be in negative k-space) and the highest bin _center_ is at `k_max`.

> For comparison, the dynasor code puts it first bin center at 0 and its last bin center at `k_max`. That means the first bin's left edge is slightly below 0 and the last bin's right edge is slightly above `k_max`. This seems appropriate enough for our Debye code, since it only uses the bin centers.
https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/issues/850#issuecomment-944799533

This test passes for `StaticStructureFactorDebye`, but not for the reason it should. The changes in b9a36120bc13ad31230f691c79fb66c39f66f582 make the `k_max` so small that `sf.bin_centers[0] == 0.0005`. That's close enough to zero that the Debye method gives `sf.S_k[0] == 999.99896`, which in turn passes the test `assert np.isclose(999.99896, 1000)`. If you change `k_max` back to 10, you get `sf.bin_centers[0] == 0.05` and `sf.S_k[0] == 989.6409`, which fails the test: `assert np.isclose(989.6409, 1000)`. That value of `k_max = 10` was deliberately chosen, and we should keep it there. There are other sets of parameters that can work, but `bins=100, k_max = 10` is a known good set and `bins=100, k_max=0.1` causes the test to pass but for the wrong reasons.

A few details to watch for:
- `allclose` is asymmetric, which has consequences:
  - 0 should be the first argument for the tilt factor check to ensure the `rtol` is applied to the potentially nonzero value (tilt factors)
  - it's probably best to check multiple directions for the box lengths, like ensuring that `Lx ~= Ly` and `Ly ~= Lx`. I used an array below to check both directions.
- floats are single precision so NumPy needs a looser `atol`, like `1e-5` instead of `1e-5` for the typical boxes we handle (order of 10^1 length units on each side)

```suggestion
        return np.allclose([self.Lx, self.Lx, self.Ly, self.Ly, self.Lz, self.Lz], [self.Ly, self.Lz, self.Lx, self.Lz, self.Lx, self.Ly], rtol=1e-5, atol=1e-5) and np.allclose(0, [self.xy, self.yz, self.xz], rtol=1e-5, atol=1e-5)
```

```python
import os
import tempfile
from pydicom.dataset import FileDataset, FileMetaDataset
from pydicom.uid import UID

# Create some temporary filenames
suffix = '.dcm'
filename_little_endian = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=suffix).name
filename_big_endian = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=suffix).name

print("Setting file meta information...")
# Populate required values for file meta information
file_meta = FileMetaDataset()
file_meta.MediaStorageSOPClassUID = UID('1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2')
file_meta.MediaStorageSOPInstanceUID = UID("1.2.3")
file_meta.ImplementationClassUID = UID("1.2.3.4")

print("Setting dataset values...")
# Create the FileDataset instance (initially no data elements, but file_meta
# supplied)
ds = FileDataset(filename_little_endian, {},
                 file_meta=file_meta, preamble=b"\0" * 128)

ds.PatientAge = "206"

from deid.config import DeidRecipe
from deid.dicom.parser import DicomParser
recipe = DeidRecipe()
recipe.deid["header"].append({'action' : 'ADD', 'field' : 'PatientAge', 'value' : 'var:new_age'})
dparser = DicomParser(ds, recipe = recipe)

dparser.parse()
ds
```

dparser = DicomParser(ds, recipe = recipe2)
dparser.parse()

ds

    def unwrap_args(service_name, region_name=None, api_version=None,

                    aws_access_key_id=None, aws_secret_access_key=None,

                       endpoint_url, aws_access_key_id, aws_secret_access_key,
                       aws_session_token, config)

            'aiobotocore.session',
            'AioSession.create_client',
            _wrapt_boto_create_client
        )

Indeed, it was working for me because I run the installation in "AllUsers" mode while you are using the "CurrentUser" mode. In your case, the scheduled task fails, because it is run under the "SYSTEM" account while certbot is accessible only to your account.

I updated the creation of the scheduled task to take into account the current installation mode:
* if it is "AllUsers", create the task to be run under "SYSTEM" account
* if it is "CurrentUser", create the task to be run under current user account

I also improved two things:
* use the MultiUser NSIS extension to require the user level (Admin), since it will handle it more  finely than the NSIS buit-in option.
* propose the "AllUsers" installation mode by default, since it should be the widest case for a system tool like certbot.

The `pulumi/pulumi` tests run _within_ the Docker container, and go through the prototypical `pulumi stack init`, `pulumi up`, ... set of scenarios. Those tests are to validate that Pulumi does all the things it needs to do within that container runtime.

The `pulumi/actions` tests cover a different use case. `pulumi/actions` is used for GitHub Actions, and is used for things like CI. So the most common use-case is simply passing the args "up --stack foo/bar". And then the entry point in the Dockerfile will run `yarn install` or `pip`, etc. to get the right dependencies and then run `pulumi`.

So specifically we want those tests to exercise the entry point for the docker container itself.

It's complicated and should be called out in some long comments, but that's the idea anyway.

For expediency Pat went and added just this set of tests. I'll send out a PR to hook up the `pulumi/actions` container, running outside of the Docker container soonish.

A follow up on this, both powershell and cmd have certain characters that have to be escaped, currently provided solution assumes that user's password won't contain such characters. I think that a better solution here would be using something that will prompt for password, e.g:
`nssm set redbot ObjectName $Env:COMPUTERNAME\$Env:USERNAME $(Read-Host "Password")`

This one would still keep the password visible (as `input("Password: ")` does in Python), but it would at least always get the password right.

There is an option to replace password characters with asterixes in `Read-Host` by using `-AsSecureString` but it would result in this awfully wrong command (or at least I couldn't find a way to make this shorter in powershell):

Initially, `setuptools` and similar packages used [`cp3_10`](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/4fa006da4bbced755463b99914029c05c61523da/setuptools/_vendor/packaging/tags.py#L723-L729) as the interpreter tag for Python 3.10. After [PEP 641](https://peps.python.org/pep-0641/) was rejected, they changed to `cp310`. This patch was included in [`setuptools==59.1.0`](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/c5a4c42d38cd0ba5ecd34977e211ae4b6865afec/setuptools/_vendor/packaging/tags.py#L466-L467). With this tag set incorrectly, `python setup.py develop` ignores wheels on Python 3.10 and tries to use the source version. That is fine for pure python libraries, but means that it will try to compile Pillow from source.

Unfortunately, the `defaults` channel provides nothing between `setuptools==58.0.4` and `setuptools==61.2.0`. The former has the problems described above and the latter has problems on Python 3.7 (see #5868). Thus, I opted to use the latest release in general and special case Python 3.7 here. When we hard-depend on Python 3.8, we can simply remove this.

The fact that `smallestOnLinkPrefix` is smallest is used under one code path, where it is not `nullptr` and we are already advertising local on-link prefix,`mIsAdvertisingLocalOnLinkPrefix == true` and we want to decide if to stop advertising or not.

Let me try to go trough each case and check the difference in behavior between current code and the changes in this PR

#### (A) If AP router is advertising a (global GUA) prefix
We already have a an on-link on infra side, so we don't need to advertise `mLocalOnLinkPrefix`.
- Current code: We may still continue to advertise `mLocalOnLinkPrefix` is it is smaller than AP's prefix and we end up with two on-link prefixes (we have a comment about this in the code). I guess it is fine to have two prefixes, but it is not not ideal.
- New code: AP's prefix wins and we don't advertise `mLocalOnLinkPrefix`

#### (B) When a BR of another Thread network advertises an on-link prefix
From our perspective (on the BR deciding whether to advertise or not), this is like the first situation (AP router advertising). We have an acceptable on-link and don't need to advertise `mLocalOnLinkPrefix`.
- Current code: We will stop advertising only if our prefix is larger one. Otherwise we continue advertising (hoping the other BRs to to back off and stop).
- New PR: We deprecate and stop advertising.

One situation I can see if multiple BRs (from different thread networks) try to compete (add their own prefix and then back off when they see the other BR's prefix). We can rely on random RA tx times (jitters) to have one win. Or we can keep the "smallest prefix win" logic.

#### (C) When a BR using old implementation that advertises random ULA on-link prefix exists in the network**
This looks and behaves similar to the previous case.

I believe a user of the `invert()` method probably does not care how the inverse is computed. I suspect that users most likely would like to get the most accurate inverse or an inverse that is "good enough" (accurate enough). For some models it is easy to have analytic inverses. For others - inverses are fundamentally numerical (series, iterations, etc.) For example, most definitely there will be no analytical inverse for `Polynomial2D` model of degree > 4. Would the user mind that the roots of polynomials of 5th degree are computed using iterative inverse? I really doubt.

What user might care about is if inverse is of limited accuracy (such as SIP-like inverse for NIRCAM that is being used now) vs iterative inverse that, in principle, can be computed to "arbitrary" accuracy.

I was thinking that if we could somehow distinguish between infinitely accurate analytic inverses and "user inverses" supplied by those who have defined forward model (=not by the caller of `invert()`) that likely have finite accuracy. If this method could distinguish between these two kind of "analytic" inverses  then there could be a logic that would select the most accurate and fastest method based on user desired accuracy for the inverse.

But if you insist to have a distinct method for the iterative (or numerical) inverse - it is fine too.

According to our investigation, latest 5.x Linux kernel supports (*, G) multicasting, meantime 4.x Linux and macOS(BSD) does not support (*, G) multicasting, but only support (S, G) multicasting. 

What MLR feature needs for the PBBR is (*, G) multicast routing, thus we need to implement (*, G) multicast routing using (S, G) multicast routing in the POSIX platform code.

Even though it's possible to use a simpler implementation using (*, G) multicast routing provided by latest Linux kernel, I think **the current implementation is valuable because it's more portable**. It works on a wide range of Linux kernel versions and can be ported to support macOS.

> design goals that are arbitrary.

Not arbitrary. Not fully explained, sure, because the design goals were generally well established so I didn't relitigate them. The design goals of this project are enumerated in https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nxPdvuzgCPKhTabhfYBN2tbcr85enSR83OpcvdfWXfY/edit#heading=h.6o3hd2sgs0dd.

tl;dr of the relevant bits to this discussion of why we have this Field-driven vs. Target-driven API:
1. Provide downstream rules a structured mechanism for requesting only the Fields they care about and ignoring others.
   * This must work with subclasses of Fields. `PythonSources` can be substituted in for `Sources`, but not vice versa.
2. Extensibility: add new fields to pre-existing target types.
   * We do this now with `UnionRule(PythonLibrary.PluginField, CustomField)`
   * We couldn't easily do this with Targets as dataclasses because Fields would be declared statically at definition time; traditionally, with dataclasses, you'd subclass something to add new attributes to it.
3. Extensibility: create custom target types.
   * You can choose to ignore fields that a spiritual superclass has. E.g. `CustomPythonLibrary` can choose to _not_ register the `Compatibility` field normally associated with `PythonLibrary`. Inheritance of `Targets` (V1 approach) would break this because inheritance makes it difficult/dangerous to deregister an attribute.

For #3, we could say "okay, don't use inheritance" to avoid that, but then we get substantial boilerplate (and still haven't solved #2):

```python
class PythonLibrary(Target):
  alias = "python_library"
  sources: Optional[PythonLibrarySources]
  compatibility: Optional[Compatbility]
  tags: Optional[Tags]
  dependencies: Optional[Dependencies]
   ...
```

vs.

```python
class PythonLibrary(Target):
  alias = "python_library"
  core_fields = (PythonLibrarySources, Compatibility, Tags, Dependencies, ...)
```

If you use a custom pyarrow filesystem, then the user needs to pass in an already stripped path, rather than a URI.

```python
(storage_filesystem=CustomS3FileSystem(), storage_path="bucket/a/b/c")
# Cannot use "s3://bucket/a/b/c", since pyarrow.fs.copy_files will complain about a URI being present
```

The default case uses `pyarrow.fs.from_uri` to strip the prefix from the path:

```python
(storage_filesystem=None, storage_path="s3://bucket/a/b/c")
# from_uri(storage_path) -> (DefaultS3FileSystem(), "bucket/a/b/c")
```

[Mutable default arguments](https://docs.python-guide.org/writing/gotchas/#mutable-default-arguments):

"""
A new list is created once when the function is defined, and the same list is used in each successive call.

Python’s default arguments are evaluated once when the function is defined, not each time the function is called (like it is in say, Ruby). This means that if you use a mutable default argument and mutate it, you will and have mutated that object for all future calls to the function as well.
"""

We could use None as a default and construct a list if it is None, but I don't see the need for ```specials``` to be a list based on the rest of the code. [A tuple is not mutable](https://docs.python.org/3.8/library/stdtypes.html#tuples), so we can use it instead.

The `delimiters` variable is set by the `-D` option by the user.

```
def choose_delimiter(delimiters):
    """
    Choose a random delimiter from the list
    """
    return rng().choice(delimiters)
```

If the user uses both the `-R` and the `-D` options, each character in the `-D` options is chosen independently. There's no way for users to specify they want to use multiple characters with random delimiters.

This is the output of the current public version.

```
> xkcdpass -n3 --min 3 --max 3 -R -D'-+*/'
+oaf-mudgab+
```

If we want to add multi-character random delimiters in the future, we can deal with it later. But I think using multiple characters for random delimiter is equivalent to using a larger `--max` option value with single character random delimiters.

> Off topic: I am not familiar about the ProtocolAdapter, but the behaviour is not consistent with SerialAdapter, but I understand this is intentional. Probably I need to read the documentation before commenting further.

The behaviour is consistent, except for the "break_on_termchar". The reason is, that for the `ProtocolAdapter` you do not specify term_chars (to be agnostic to term_chars), therefore, the Adapter cannot recognize a termchar and cannot stop there.
However, you give the communication to the Adapter, so reading two times up to a termchar would require the following `comm__pairs = [(None, b"part one"), (None, b"part two")]` for an instrument returning `b"part one\npart two\n"` with newline as term_char.

Setting a global random seed would lead to a lot of confusion about to reproduce a configuration.  If the seed is set once on startup, then all the actions that depend on the RNG must be called in the *exact* same order otherwise the configuration will be different.  Whats complicates things more is suppose that you don't want to call something like 'PutObject' (which has randomization) for a particular scene setup, then you basically won't be able to have the same seed for a subsequent call to another action that the RNG depends on.  As well, if the seed is set once for the Player, then all training, scene loading, etc. must occur in exactly the same order and each action must make the exact same number of calls to the RNG or a subsequent's action won't receive the same sequence of random numbers.

> In general, I think we should be careful about trying to "fix" Git rather than just augmenting it for the multi-repo stuff.

We should not try to fix `git`, and we should not try to assume what the user wants.
When I do a `get fetch`, `git pull`, `git rebase`, `git merge`, and `git merge --no-ff`, I know exactly what they'll do to my repo, and where those commands needs help.

As a starter to `west`, I don't know that `west` will do a rebase and abort, and thereafter I need to use `git` manually.
And we don't know if user expect their project to end in a non-working state.
We don't know if he wants to preserve / revert his changes.
Therefore I think the best is to assume nothing, and leave the repo's as-is. (ie. rolling back)

> A lot of thought has gone into how it works, and we probably can't do better if we try. Its behavior makes sense ("hey, I couldn't update this thing, but I'll give you what I could do and let you fix the rest"). It's what people used to Git expect. We shouldn't surprise them too much, because losing them would be worse than losing the people who can't be bothered to learn a tiny bit of Git.

Is it what people expects ?
I would say that we should try  not to touch peoples local work, unless users request so.
If there later comes a request, where people would like `west` to leave the repo in partially rebased state, then we can try this.
Until then, I think we should only rebase if it can be done cleanly.

> The Git guys already thought of this, with git pull --ff-only. It even aborts if the working tree is unclean, etc.

This sounds nicer, except that if user has local commits, he is forced to merge / rebase those changes himself, even if a rebase could be applied cleanly.
But in that case, the user is working with west or manifest development, and I think it is fine that he has to use git directly for that.
Most important feature for `west` is to make it easier for those who develops on Zephyr multi repo itself.
Those maintaining west or manifest repos may be considered little more advanced users ;-)

Only question left then is:
- What to do if `west` updates successfully but `manifest` fails ?
Rollback both ?

It's not clear to me that the 'no such attribute' error case is correct, in either the C or Python versions. 

For `repr`, in both implementations, if the lookup fails, we simply return `repr(aq_inner(self))` (aka `self->obj`) which goes through some complicated CPython internal logic. All good, won't fail.

For `__unicode__` (which only matters on Python 2), the C implementation catches the error and defers to the `str()` implementation, which is what CPython does implicitly for objects implemented in Python, I think (which is why the Python implementation doesn't do anything special).

For `__bytes__`, the C implementation will catch an error and instead call `str(self)`. That *might* work on Python 2, where str-is-bytes, but on Python 3, where str-is-unicode, it might fail, not least because of `str` having non-ASCII characters. Does CPython ordinarily do that automatically in Python 3? Let's check:

```pycon
$ python3.7
Python 3.7.3 (default, Mar 30 2019, 03:37:43)
[Clang 10.0.0 (clang-1000.11.45.5)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> class WithBytes:
...     def __bytes__(self):
...         return b'my bytes'
...     def __str__(self):
...         return u'my snowman: \u2603'
...
>>> bytes(WithBytes())
b'my bytes'
>>> class WithoutBytes:
...     def __str__(self):
...         return u'my snowman: \u2603'
...
>>> bytes(WithoutBytes())
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

Perhaps the solution here is to pass in the `InnerGraph` and have this `clear` method remove edges? Perhaps the solution is to modify `Graph::clear` to remove the edges (and add a caveat to the documentation of `clear` that it expects the caller to remove any edges to this `Entry`)... Yeah, the audience and scope are part of the way we support third-party logins.  Basically when the frontend (be that tc-ui or treeherder or releng-services) requests an auth0 login, it indicates an additional audience and scope that correspond to an auth0 "API" object which we asked the IAM team to create.  When Auth0 sees this, it presents a consent dialog saying "do you want to grant treeherder access to Taskcluster?"  The result is an access token that has two audiences (treeherder and Taskcluster), and both of those services can rely on it to authenticate the user.  So this is how we support third-party logins.

It's extra work to get the IAM team to set this up, and since we don't want to support this method of third-party logins, it's probably not worth it.

However, we do still need to verify that the access_token was meant for us - that is, that we are the intended audience!  That is done by checking that the audience is equal to the auth0 client id.  Without this, I could steal your TC credentials by:
 * setting up some toy Mozilla site (maybe another Bugzilla dashboard!)
 * configuring auth0 for that site
 * getting you to login
 * taking the resulting access_token and calling tc-web-server's `getCredentials` query with it

Additionally to the important requirement not to use /tmp, i have a small suggestion:

After a successful visudo test on the generated sudoers snippet you should not move the temp sudoers-file, you should rather write the `$CONTENT` to the final destination `/etc/sudoers.d/$FILE_NAME`. This way you can avoid problems, in case the temporary file changed without intent, or was modified with malicious intent.

A possible rewrite with the above in mind:

```
umask 077
checksudoers=$(mktemp sshuttle-sudoers-rule.XXXXXXXXX)
[ -z "${checksudoers}" ] && echo "$CONTENT" > "${checksudoers}"

if [ visudo -c -q -f "${checksudoers}" ]; then
	if [ -e "/etc/sudoers.d/$FILE_NAME" ]; then
		rm "/etc/sudoers.d/$FILE_NAME"
	fi
	echo "$CONTENT" > "/etc/sudoers.d/$FILE_NAME"
	chmod 0440 "/etc/sudoers.d/$FILE_NAME"
	rm "${checksudoers}"
	exit 0
else
	visudo -c -f "${checksudoers}"
	rm "${checksudoers}"
	exit 1
fi
```

```python
class MainCallback(Callback):
    """
    MainCallback is a one-of-a-kind callback that contains hook functions:
        - `on_iter_forward`
        - `on_iter_backward`
        - `on_lr_adjust`

    These methods are somewhat special, because only one special MainCallback
    should be allowed to implement these methods among all callbacks, otherwise
    there will propagate forward and backward twice.
    """
    def on_iter_forward(self, runner):
        """
        If `on_train_forward` and `on_val_forward` are not overridden,
        this will be called during forward when training and validating.
        Any behavior inconsistent with the default forward behavior should be overridden here.
        """
        # unpack features into features and targets
        *features, target = runner.batch
        runner.target = target
        # Forward features
        runner.output = runner.model(*features)
        # Ensure `targetL` and `outputL` are always in a list format.
        targetL = [target] if not isinstance(target, (list, tuple)) else target
        outputL = [runner.output] if not isinstance(runner.output, (list, tuple)) else runner.output
        # Compute loss
        runner.loss = runner.criterion(*outputL, *targetL)
```

For a bit of background: 
Previously (that was several years ago), the `tempfile` module was patched in `pyfakefs`, but the patch was incomplete and had problems, and broke with every new Python version. So I removed it from patching (together with `glob` which had a similar problem) and solely relied on the patched `os` / `os.path` functionality. That worked, except that `tempfile` assumes an existing temp directory, otherwise it crashes, so I decided to create that directory on startup.

It is true that this changes the behavior [if you expected an empty filesystem](https://pytest-pyfakefs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/troubleshooting.html#os-temporary-directories), but so far I had no reports that this causes big problems.

I was also not sure about the usage pattern of `pyfakefs`. Recently I did some statistics with the repositories shown as dependent on `pyfakefs` (as this is the only source I have for that), counting only repositories that had at least 100 stars (about 50, so not really a large sample), and found that of these, more than 40% used it via `pytest`, and about the same number via the `unittest` support added by @jmcgeheeiv. Another 5% used the patcher directly, and the rest (4 in this case) had been manually patching as originally used by Google.
If assume that the usage you are referring to is inside Google, which is good to know. 

More to the point: I could add an argument to the fake filesystem initialization (defaulting to `False`) that would control if the temp file shall be created. I would probably set that to `True` if initialized from the patcher - this way, it would retain the current behavior if invoking `pyfakefs` via `pytest` or `unittest`, and would also retain the behavior if creating it manually. Would that be ok for you?

I can embellish in a comment, but in the meantime, here's an explanation:

### Quick summary of dataclass feature

The design and implementation is inspired and based on the built-in dataclass feature, where you can define classes like:

```python
@dataclass
class C:
    x: int
    y: int = field(repr=False)
    z: int = field(repr=False, default=10)
    t: int = 20
```

Relevant quotes from https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html#dataclasses.dataclass:

> The dataclass() decorator examines the class to find `field`s. A `field` is defined as class variable that has a [type annotation](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-variable-annotation).
>
> [...]
>
> fields may optionally specify a default value, using normal Python syntax:
>
> ```python
> @dataclass
> class C:
>    a: int       # 'a' has no default value
>    b: int = 0   # assign a default value for 'b'
>```

And https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html#dataclasses.field:

> For common and simple use cases, no other functionality is required. There are, however, some dataclass features that require additional per-field information. To satisfy this need for additional information, you can replace the default field value with a call to the provided [`field()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html#dataclasses.field) function. For example:
>
> ```python
> @dataclass
> class C:
>     mylist: List[int] = field(default_factory=list)
> 
> c = C()
> c.mylist += [1, 2, 3]
> ```
>
> [...]
>
> If the default value of a field is specified by a call to `field()`, then the class attribute for this field will be replaced by the specified `default` value. If no `default` is provided, then the class attribute will be deleted. The intent is that after the `dataclass()` decorator runs, the class attributes will all contain the default values for the fields, just as if the default value itself were specified. For example, after:
>
> ```python
> @dataclass
> class C:
>     x: int
>     y: int = field(repr=False)
>     z: int = field(repr=False, default=10)
>     t: int = 20
> ```
>
> The class attribute `C.z` will be `10`, the class attribute `C.t` will be `20`, and the class attributes `C.x` and `C.y` will not be set.

## Our design

We're allowing output types to be specified in a similar way:

```
@pulumi.output_type
class Foo:
    my_arg: str = pulumi.property("myArg")
    some_other_value: bool = pulumi.property("someOtherValue")
```

Dataclasses uses "fields" as its terminology, but for us, I'm referring to these as "properties". Our "properties" can similarly be defined as class variables with type annotations. Our `pulumi.property()` function is analogous to the `dataclasses.field()` function, and is used to provide additional information associated with the "property": in this case, the Pulumi camelCase name (pun, omg, 😆).
	
We first determine the class's "properties" in `_properties_from_annotations()` by looking at the class's `__annotations__` (which is the dict `{"my_arg": str, "some_other_value": bool}` for the example above) and then look up the class attribute value for each key in that dict. In this example, the value of the `my_arg` class attribute is an instance of `_Property(name="myArg")`, and the value of `some_other_value` is `_Property(name="someOtherValue")`. We do some more massaging, store the type annotation in the `_Property` instance, and then `_properties_from_annotations()` returns a dictionary of the class attribute names to `_Property` instances.

Then the decorator "cleans up" the class, by removing any of the class attributes that were set to `_Property` via a call to `pulumi.property()`.

Dataclasses does the same thing, cleaning up the class attributes that were set to values returned from calls to `field()`.

```python
new = []
for p in principal_list:
    if p.startswith("urn:"):
        new.append(p)
        continue
    resolved = auth_client.maybe_lookup_identity_id(p)
    if resolved:
        new.append(f"urn:globus:auth:identity:id:{resolved}"
    else:
        raise click.UsageError("bad, no such user")
```

OK I took the wrong examples with plugins, forgotting that plugins do not call certbot, is it certbot that call plugins through the dynamic plugins discovery mechanism of python.

That was not the point I wanted to enlight. 

Monkeypatching a standard library is not a question about design, it is a question about wrong code. If you monkeypatch the standard library, it does not make it effective only for your own code, but for any code that use yours.

It means that any external developper, for any project and any context, import certbot package in one of its own module, will see a standard module be silently swapped to a custom on, starting from the first import of the certbot package, and for the rest of the runtime execution.

The consequences are widely unknown, completely out of our range. It is just not an option. We can discuss ways to ease usage of this specific module, but monkeypatching is not a solution.

Apart that, developers are not required to understand the compat.os module. It is an implementation detail that claim externally as a contract to be a valid replacement of the standard os module for certbot. I do not think that it is overcomitting, as it does not add a single line compared to a call to the standard os, and that a CI process is able to do the job to check this specific subtility. Compared with the thousands of unit test out there, and all the dynamic mocks called from them, for me it is not a big deal ^^

I realize you already did a bunch of work here, but I'm a little bit uneasy about incorporating such sophisticated and conceptually nondeterministic [1] testing of what is ultimately very, very thin wrappers over `random`.  This feels like a case where it's better to just test against a mock implementation and ensure that the wrappers call the expected APIs, and then trust that those APIs behave as documented.  Otherwise I feel like we're picking up a not-insubstantial maintenance burden for the test code itself, which seems out of proportion to the complexity of the implementation.

The other concern I have is that this test, while elegant, wouldn't even catch many types of non-uniform behavior:
- we're only testing each method with a fixed range; non-uniformity that doesn't appear within this range goes undetected.  For example, a not-strictly-uniform implementation using `rand() % N` works for range widths that evenly divide `RAND_MAX` but not otherwise.
- we're only testing that buckets above a minimum threshold of 0.95*expected and not checking against a maximum threshold, so a non-uniformity that assigns 5% of the probability mass to a single bucket still passes, even in a case of 100 buckets where that makes the single bucket 5 times more likely than any other
- we aren't testing against the buckets being *too* uniform; for the discrete domains, deterministic uniformity (e.g. a stateful round-robin sampling approach) would pass this test with flying colors, but it's contrary to the presumed probabilistic behavior which we'd expect to actually look a little non-uniform some of the time.

So I'm worried this test gives a false sense of comprehensiveness.

[1]  Even if they are functionally deterministic in the particular invocation here due to the fixed seed, there's nothing about the logic that guarantees that; the 0 seed just "happens" to work, so the fact that the test passes can't really be ascertained via inspection alone.

`_transposeByInstrument` essentially gets instruments and then transposes. It doesn't recurse. Tranposing recurses, sure, but the instrument search does not recurse. The instrument search is done with `extendDurationsAndGetBoundaries('Instrument')`, which is marked for deprecation, so maybe it's time to rewrite the instrument search and then drop the recursing from toSoundingPitch/toWrittenPitch, which will simplify them even further.

Another benefit of doing that is that it will prevent edge cases from there being superfluous instruments at multiple levels. Bb Clarinet in part and Bb Clarinet in measure == Clarinet in Ab?

Another another benefit is that the helper method to treatAtSoundingPitch won't have to be called on non-self objects and so it can stay private.

Hmm actually @rkooo567, I hadn't thought of this, but I don't think that we're handling concurrent restoration requests from different nodes for the same object correctly. Consider this case (which I think can happen):

1. Node 1 spills object.
2. Node 2 issues remote restoration request to node 1.
3. Concurrently, node 1 issues a local restoration request.
4. The callbacks for one of these restoration requests will not be invoked due to deduping (with the current implementation), causing one of these pulls to never succeed.

With your suggestion (adding a RAY_CHECK here), one of those restoration requests would trip that check and cause a crash.

Am I right that this can happen? If so, we'd probably want restoration requests from different nodes to join on a single restoration that's processed, where each callback is invoked once the restoration is done, right?

So this makes sense and is great, but since it's my problem I have to ask: I'm really afraid of doing stuff like this when, in the near term, it's very possible that the parent and child resources could be created in two separate processes, in two separate languages. It seems like we'd basically be screwed if this were the case:

1. "Remote" children of resources would not be able to register themselves with the parent, since that involves mutating an object in another process,
2. Parents who create "remote" resources won't be aware of them, since it's the child's responsibility to add itself to the list of child resources in the parent

In my mind this imposes a heavy constraint on a multi-language interop story: we actually need to remote resource objects and expose them as "handles", and both the caller and the callee language host need to be able to call into each other at-will. Furthermore, we'll have to be absolutely sure that every language implements this the exact same way, since it would be possible for children to remote into a parent implementation in a different language while still expecting the host language's semantics.

I love the idea here, it makes sense to me, but I do wonder if it's possible to store this state in a different location. Perhaps in the engine? Some way such that we have a well-defined, language-independent protocol of accessing the set of resources that are parented to a particular resource?

This sort-of depends on our strategy for multi-language interop, so I get that it's hard to worry about hypothetical issues for a system that doesn't yet exist, but I worry that this is going to work great for Node and be a massive headache as soon as we start moving into a multi-process world.

I don't want to put it there because the following scenario is possible:
1. p.subscribe("foo")
2. health check is performed
3. p.unsubscribe("foo")
4. a health check response still hasn't received
5. p.unsuscribe("foo") 
6. clean_health_check_response is being called by the unsubscribe command, the health check  response hasn't arrived yet and it exists the loop due to ttl runs-out
7. the health check response only now received 
8. p.subscribe() is being called - self.subscribed is still False so a health check will be performed and we should clean the existing health check response before we continue. 
9. If we add 'clean_health_check_response=0' at the end of clean_health_check, we will clean the counter in step 6, so we won't be able to clean the socket from the response on step 8.

Although in that case, my thought was that the process execution options should be a second struct because they differ in focus from `RemotingOptions`. Specifically, the process execution config focuses on execution strategy between local and remote, while `RemotingOptions` focuses on configuring remote execution.

Moreover, `ExecutionOptions` on the Python side would go away and the Python code would just directly instantiate the `PyRemotingOptions` and `PyExecutionStrategyOptions`. This would be beneficial because it would reduce duplicate data definitions but also allow putting the defaults into Rust code closer to the places they are used. (Then the Python<->Rust layer exposes `PyRemotingOptions::default` as a `default` class method used by the options system.

I just did a bit of research on this. From what I've gathered, adding `__all__` wouldn't make a difference here. You could still import it with:

```
 from django_ses.utils import  _CERT_CACHE 
```

The only thing that using `__all__` affects is whether it's imported by this:

```
from django_ses.utils import *
```

But if you begin the object's name with an underscore, as we have here, it's not imported by `*` anyway. So, I don't think adding `__all__`  would even do what we'd hoped.

So far we have avoided dealing with the `Chart.lock` file but some sort of implementation would be needed.

This puts forward the question of whether we want Pants to manage Helm's 3rd party dependencies or not. In Helm, the `dependencies` of a given chart are always _3rd party_, meaning that they are supposed to come from published repositories while first party dependencies (subcharts) are meant to be put inside the `charts/` folder inside the given chart.

In our experience this is somewhat messy, specially if you have a few customs charts of `library` type which are meant to be used by other, more complex, charts as it forces you to go through the `package->publish->generate-lockfiles` flow whenever you make a change to the smaller charts.

In our implementation what we do is parsing `Chart.yaml` file and infer any dependencies from it, whether they are first- or third- party, then in the rule logic that returns `HelmChart` type, we package all those subcharts under the `charts/` folder and re-render the `Chart.yaml` including the first party dependencies in it.

With that approach we avoid of publishing the first party dependencies before being able to run any `lint` or `test` goals in the other charts that reuse those.

In theory it should be possible to find a solution that satisfies both, like copying into the `charts/` folder any first party dependencies and then use `helm dependency update`/`helm dependency build` commands to let Helm resolve the third party dependencies based on the Chart.yaml.

> May I ask why do we need to support the case that `theme` is string?

Because everything passed to CLI via the shell will be treated as `str`.

Moreover, the `value`s of an `Enum` are usually basic types like `str` or `int`. Using `dict` as the value, like

```py
class Theme(Enum):
    DARK = THEME_DARK
    LIGHT = THEME_LIGHT
    NONE = THEME_NONE
```

will result in:
```
> az demo style -h

Arguments
    --theme            : The theme to format styled text.  Allowed values: {<Style.PRIMARY:
                         'primary'>: '\x1b[39m', <Style.SECONDARY: 'secondary'>: '\x1b[90m',
                         <Style.IMPORTANT: 'important'>: '\x1b[35m', <Style.ACTION: 'action'>:
                         '\x1b[34m', <Style.HYPERLINK: 'hyperlink'>: '\x1b[36m', <Style.ERROR:
                         'error'>: '\x1b[31m', <Style.SUCCESS: 'success'>: '\x1b[32m',
                         <Style.WARNING: 'warning'>: '\x1b[33m'}, {<Style.PRIMARY: 'primary'>:
                         '\x1b[39m', <Style.SECONDARY: 'secondary'>: '\x1b[90m', <Style.IMPORTANT:
                         'important'>: '\x1b[95m', <Style.ACTION: 'action'>: '\x1b[94m',
                         <Style.HYPERLINK: 'hyperlink'>: '\x1b[96m', <Style.ERROR: 'error'>:
                         '\x1b[91m', <Style.SUCCESS: 'success'>: '\x1b[92m', <Style.WARNING:
                         'warning'>: '\x1b[93m'}, {}.  Default: dark.
```

Lets consider the concequences.  We have a return of type X but the actual type is Y.  We try to build a wrapper for type Y but get an exception so we instead return wrapper X.  In Java this isnt an issue as the static type is X and they would have to cast, but in Python there is only the dynamic type Y that is expected.

If X is ``Driver`` and Y is ``DrillImpl`` then all makes sense because X is a useable class.  Where is gets ugly is what is X is Object?  Now it gets ugly.  Does the user have to cast or do we go to the super type?  What if the supertype is Object as all the class has is an Interface?  What if it has multiple interfaces?

The problem is that Java public classes no longer mean public.  Many times implementation classes are public just so they can be created from the package due to the lack of a friend concept.  Thus we cant tell is Y is really a public class or a private class which is public due to module access.

Returning the static type all the time is not an option as operating on a list would be a pain and very unpythonic.

It seems like an error is the safest, but there is no way to avoid the error and just get the base wrapper.  Thus we end up stuck as bad jars become completely unusable.

This is a hard problem.  No option is particularly good.

It's not ideal, but we can circumvent this limitation and add additional dependencies through the `synth.py` file. That way the changes to `noxfile.py` will not get lost on the next code regen.

Applying the following patch should do the trick:

```patch
diff --git noxfile.py noxfile.py
index 3af3d67..20b5ad2 100644
--- noxfile.py
+++ noxfile.py
@@ -70,14 +70,12 @@ def lint_setup_py(session):
 
 def default(session):
     # Install all test dependencies, then install this package in-place.
+
+    session.install("mock", "pytest", "pytest-cov")
     session.install(
-        "mock",
-        "pytest",
-        "pytest-cov",
-        "opentelemetry-api",
-        "opentelemetry-sdk",
-        "opentelemetry-instrumentation",
+        "opentelemetry-api", "opentelemetry-sdk", "opentelemetry-instrumentation",
     )
+
     session.install("-e", ".")
 
     # Run py.test against the unit tests.
diff --git synth.py synth.py
index 0e2c96e..10edae8 100644
--- synth.py
+++ synth.py
@@ -279,4 +279,18 @@ s.move(templated_files)
 # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
 python.py_samples()
 
+# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# Additional unit test dependencies
+# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
+s.replace(
+    "noxfile.py",
+    r'session\.install\("mock", "pytest", "pytest-cov"\)',
+    """
+    \g<0>
+    session.install(
+        "opentelemetry-api", "opentelemetry-sdk", "opentelemetry-instrumentation",
+    )
+    """,
+)
+
 s.shell.run(["nox", "-s", "blacken"], hide_output=False)
```

`requires_encryption` is sort of an odd option. In almost all cases its mostly ignored and whether we encrypt/decrypt is controlled by whether the user passes `key_encryption_key` or `key_resolver_function` on client construction. Even if you set this to `False` but provide a `key_encryption_key`, we would still perform encryption.

The only case this value is taken into account is if the blob isn't actually encrypted (no encryption metadata is found) or there is an error in parsing the metadata. If `requires_encryption` is `True` these cases will cause an exception, if its `False`, no exception will occur, and the blob will be treated as unencrypted.

So to actually answer your question, no I don't think we need/want to assess that value here since if someone sets this to `True` for some reason but doesn't provide the other arguments, we don't want to make this extra request.

There are 4 points for me to use `std::vector` instead.

1. I find it is not convenient to use `std::unique_ptr<uint8_t[]>` in general cases with `boost::asio`. `boost::asio` has native support for `std::vector` as a buffer. To use `std::unique_ptr<uint8_t[]>` with `boost::asio`, we need to use `get()` to extract the underlying raw pointer, and pass the length of the buffer as an independent parameter. In all other places, we have replaced `std::unique_ptr<uint8_t[]>` with `std::vector`.

2. For our general message processing, we need a buffer with a length for the purpose of various data parsing (flatbuffer, msgpack, protobuf, etc). For me `std::vector` is a better abstraction with some boundary guarantee.

3. The performance is not a problem, because we need to allocating memory for `std::unique_ptr<uint8_t[]>` dynamically too, and we use `reserve` for `std::vector`.

4. Google C++ style prefers pointer type for `output arguments`. This creates ugly types like `std::unique_ptr<uint8_t[]>*`. I really don't like it. Why not just use managed buffers like `std::vector` to control the lifetime? C++ should ensure zero-cost abstraction.

That's a good point. Since ML-EID is a unicast address, we need to support at least 3 MA for each Child. 
 If a parent has maximum 10 Children, it will needs to have at least 30 proxied Multicast Subscriptions (otherwise it's a violation of Thread Spec.) .
Meanwhile, we can't reduce the size of Address list of the Child because we still need to support 4 Unicast Addresses. 

I think having to support `ChildrenNum * 3`  Multicast Subscriptions (19B per) can be a burden for routers. 

From such viewpoint, moving Multicast Addresses to this table can not bring us memory optimization. But the Child will be able to subscribe to a lot of Multicast Addresses. But subscribing to so many Multicast Addresses might not be a common use case for Thread networks. 

That being said, my opinion would be these:
- Using a separate table for proxying multicast addresses have significant performance advantage over using addresses in `Child`. 
- Storing a copy of multicast address on `Netif` to this table can make the implementation of `MlrManager` simpler, but it's less efficient in memory usage than using addersses in Netif directly. **But if the locally subscribed multicast address is also subscribed by a Child (proxied), there is no additional memory cost for copying it from Netif.**
- I expect the Thread nodes are subscribing to the same set of multicast addresses in a common setup. In such case, this table would contain addresses that are both locally subscribed and proxied, which is a very efficient use of memory.

1. I understand your point of wanting to make minimal changes and keep logic where it is. However, in this case, I'd like to ask you to reconsider -- let me explain why! Currently, the token classes do the parsing and some processing, and the renderer classes process the input, too. It would be helpful if the token classes would limit themselves to only do the parsing, and leave all processing to the renderers -- because different renderers may have different needs. I'm working on a Markdown renderer, for example, where the goal is to be able to do a near-perfect round trip. That's not possible if the source has been processed in an irreversible way. (I am nearly finished with a first iteration of that renderer, and it does have a few more little source-preserving changes like this one.) I am of course fully aware that mistletoe was not written with "my" use case in mind, but I do believe that a clear separation of concerns between parsing and rendering can benefit the project as a whole anyway.

```
class A:
    i = 1
    l = []

a1 = A()
a2 = A()

a1.i = 2
print(a2.i)

a1.l.append("whoooops")
print(a2.l)
```

1
['whoooops']

- We put a cache dir in the `service` directory for each tox environment.
- Within CI, we discover the targeted packages, figure out which tox envs are skipped, etc, and come up with a set of `tox` invocations. One for each package in the service that is triggering CI.
- Invoke all tox environments for a given package in parallel, iterating across all packages that are contained within a service directory.
- This means that sharing at the `service` level on a `per environment` basis, we avoid parallel access issues while _also_ ensuring that we don't download fresh dependencies on each tox environment (past the first one).

With `from eth_abi import decode`, the user is getting an instance of `ABIDecoder.decode`, which then builds a registry of the various decoder classes needed for the types passed in the first argument.

The stream object can't be passed directly to `StringDecoder`'s `decode` function - it has to go through a `HeadTailDecoder` first to handle the padding.

I manually tested this looping solution . The `errors` argument is sticky from one `decode` call to the next, so having it default to `None` doesn't work, but by having it default to `strict` I can get the desired behavior of only overriding if `errors` is provided by the user.

91e5bd48a

First of all, I moved the ID clash check in add_file to the top. If ID clashes and neither `force` nor `ignore` are active, refuse to add outright.

I also got rid of the "reuse existing OcrdFile" mechanism altogether because it offers no benefit and can lead to hard-to-debug errors. Instead, in case of clashes and with `force` active, existing files will be removed first, before being added. No change in behavior for non-clashing files.

AFAICS there are two sets of files where straightforward clash detection won't work: a) AlternativeImages (which might be many with the same pageId, mimetype and filegrp) and b) document-wide documents like TEI or PDF that do not have a pageId.

To accomodate a), I've added a new kwarg `is_alternative_image` that, if set, will ignore the "grp/mimetype/pageId" check outright. Unless the IDs are clashing, it's perfectly valid to have many files with the same mimetype in the same fileGrp for the same pageId.

The files in b) have in common that they lack a pageId. So, in case there is a "grp/mimetype" clash without `pageId` being set, nothing is removed. This does prevent e.g. having two vartiants of TEI of the whole document in the same fileGrp. So if that is a realistic prospect, it needs another round of adaptions.

I **think** the decision tree in `OcrdMets.add_file` should largely reflect your proposal, with the added complication of handling `force`, `ignore`, `is_alternative_image` and "is a file without pageId" as described.

> Is "view content" meant to imply a Learner display versus e.g. viewing analytics or metadata?

Yes

> If it's always Learn, does it imply a specific learning display modality? (self-contained/isolated vs contextualized in a channel, lesson, etc)?

No - it is up to the individual plugin to determine how the redirect should be resolved. In the case of learn, if we wanted to get smart, we could try to see if that content item existed in any lessons currently assigned to the logged in user and redirect based on that. I have not done that, but the hook is unopinionated about whether this should happen or not.

> Will we ever want to parameterize 'view content' further to provide more specific instructions?

Yes - future versions could take additional GET params, but allow the fallback behaviour encoded here if that Kolibri version does not support these, or the higher specificity is not locally available.

> Do we expect to re-use this pattern for other content or DB model types, e.g. channel, facility, user, etc?

No - I don't think so. Content is different because it plays a very different role across the Kolibri ecosystem and appears consistently in many different Kolibri installations. It is also fundamentally public, whereas most of the other data in Kolibri is private to more or lesser a degree.

Channels might be one other place where we might offer a universal redirect, but I don't have a use case in mind for why yet.

> Are there situations where multiple plugins might legitimately register handlers for the same or similar URL patterns?

Assuming I am understanding this correctly - this is possible, but as all registered plugin urls are namespaced under the plugin name, the plugins would have to be named identically for this to occur (but we should also complain noisily - we don't currently - if this happens).

```
def fedis(aoi, n=1.5, n_ref=None):

    iam_physical = physical(aoi, n, K=0, L=0)

    if n_ref is None:
        return iam_physical

    else:
        # reflectance for normal incidence with refractive index n:
        rd0 = ((n - 1) / (n + 1)) ** 2

        # reflectance for normal incidence with refractive index n_ref:
        r0 =  ((n_ref - 1) / (n_ref + 1)) ** 2

        # weighting function
        w = (1 - rd0) / (1 - r0)
        
        return w * iam_physical
```

Let's make the following updates to this function:
1. Rename it to `create_client()`. The added descriptions to the function name make it hard to follow.
2. Turn the `create_client()` function into a pytest fixture that yields back a factory to create clients. This will allow us to keep the temporary file and environment patch around for the length of the test instead of have it end when the client is returned
3. Create the session as part of the factory. This will allow us to avoid patching the `configprovider` module

So in the end, I'd imagine it would look roughly like this:
```python
@pytest.fixture
def create_client(tmp_path):
    tmp_config_file = tmp_path / config
    environ = {'AWS_CONFIG_FILE': tmp_config_file}
    def _do_create_client(service, profile, client_args=None, config_file_contents=None, environment=None):
         environ.update(environment)
         # Write contents to tmp config file here
         session = botocore.session.Session(profile=profile)
         return client = session.create_client(service, **client_args)

    with mock.patch('os.envrion', environ)
        yield _do_create_client
```

Correct, the design here is precomputing and caching distances because of their common use. I did some very loose calculations/benchmarking and decided this was the right tradeoff (and that the additional memory consumption was irrelevant). Feel free to check me on that, but my recollection is that a C++ cached array of distances is best to precompute because if you get to the point where it is needed, you’re usually in a TBB parallel operation where it would be difficult to compute the whole array AND if you compute the distance at that point, it causes issues with centralizing the floating point correctness. We used to have sqrt’s littered through the various methods’ implementations (when we computed both neighbor vector and distance on the fly) and the conventions and casting were wacky and subtlely different across the code base (not all even used the dot function for the dot product iirc?). That was not good. Having a single source for precomputed distances was better than the previous mess.
